source.title: Pain: A Precision Signal for Reinforcement Learning and Control

confidence_rationale: Seymour (2019) establishes pain as a precision-weighted prediction-error signal within a reinforcement-learning / active-inference framework. This is compatible with MECH-256's residual-comparator framing but operates at a higher computational level: MECH-256 proposes a per-step forward-model residual on a specific latent stream (z_harm_s), while Seymour frames pain as the global prediction-error signal that drives behavioural learning and control across timescales. Mapping fidelity is moderate -- the frameworks are architecturally convergent (both use prediction-error logic) but differ in scope (MECH-256: per-step, stream-specific; Seymour: multi-timescale, distributed). Transfer risk is low (theoretical framework with strong empirical anchoring). Confidence capped at 0.61 because Seymour's framework does not directly test or confirm the efference-copy comparator mechanism on a per-step basis -- it provides a higher-level computational motivation.
failure_signatures: Seymour frames pain as a learned-expectation prediction error, not as a per-step forward-model residual from motor efference copy -- if the efference-copy mechanism were the PRIMARY driver of self-pain attenuation, Seymour's framework would need substantial refinement to accommodate within-trial (single-step) timescales.

The precision-signal framework emphasises the role of descending modulation in shaping EXPECTED precision of pain signals (prior over precision), which is more consistent with PAG/RVM contextual gating than with the motor-efference-copy comparator -- the two mechanisms are parallel in the biological system.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Pain is a precision-weighted prediction error signal that serves as a teaching signal for reinforcement learning and as a control signal for short-term avoidance. The brain's expectation of harm (learned prior) shapes the precision (reliability) assigned to nociceptive signals. Deviation from expected harm = pain experience. Descending modulation acts by adjusting precision priors, not by cancelling predicted signals.
mapping.ree_translation: The Seymour framework maps to REE at two levels. (1) SD-029 directly: z_harm_s residual = z_harm_s_observed - E2_harm_s(z_harm_s_{t-1}, a_actual) is a per-step prediction error on the harm stream, which Seymour's framework endorses as the correct computational role for harm signals -- they ARE prediction errors that drive update. (2) The broader active-inference / precision-weighting layer maps to the PAG/RVM contextual gain control identified by De Preter & Heinricher (2024): expected precision is modulated by behavioural state (stress-analgesia, opioid tone) separately from the per-step efference-copy residual. These are two architecturally distinct but co-existing mechanisms in REE's harm stream.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Seymour explicitly argues against a purely sensory / modular view of pain. However, he does not distinguish between (a) forward-model efference-copy prediction and (b) contextual learned-expectation prediction -- both reduce pain prediction error, but through different substrate pathways and timescales. SD-029 commits to mechanism (a); Seymour's framework is agnostic between (a) and (b) at the implementation level. The convergence is at the computational (Marr level 2) rather than algorithmic (level 3) level.
mapping.source_context: Single-author theoretical review in Neuron by Seymour (Cambridge Computational and Biological Learning Lab). Computational/theoretical paper grounded in human and animal RL/pain empirical literature. Not an empirical study of self-generated vs external pain.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high (Neuron, influential theoretical paper with 300+ citations). Mapping fidelity moderate: the framework is computationally convergent with MECH-256 but at a higher level of abstraction. The paper motivates the prediction-error framing but does not directly validate the per-step efference-copy mechanism that SD-029 specifies.
